1: kai egeneto en mia tōn hēmerōn didasontos autou ton laon en tō hierō kai euangelizomenou — one of those days as he was teaching the people in the temple and proclaiming the good news — euangelizomenos (proclaiming good news) paired with didaskontos (teaching): the twin activities of Jesus's Jerusalem ministry. epestēsan hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis syn tois presbyterois — the chief priests and the scribes with the elders stood before him — the formal approach of the Jerusalem Sanhedrin: archiereis (high priests), grammateis (scribes), and presbyteroi (elders) constitute the three components of the Sanhedrin (cf. Mark 14:53; Acts 4:5). Their standing (epestēsan) before him in the temple court is an official confrontation.
2: kai eipan legontes pros auton, Eipov hēmin, en poia exousia tauta poieis, ē tis estin ho dous soi tēn exousian tautēn — they said to him: tell us by what authority you do these things, or who is the one who gave you this authority — the double-form of the question covers both the question of kind (en poia exousia = in what kind of authority) and the question of source (tis estin ho dous = who gave it). The temple cleansing (Luke 19:45-46) and the continuing teaching are both in view. Exousia (authority) — the same word that amazed the synagogue in Capernaum (Luke 4:32); now the Jerusalem leadership demands its authorization. Without rabbinic credentials, a priestly appointment, or official commission, Jesus's authority was unexplained by normal categories.
3: apokritheis de eipen pros autous, Erōtēsō hymas kagō logon, kai eipate moi — he answered and said to them: I also will ask you a word; tell me — the counter-question is the Socratic-rabbinic device of answering a question with a question. Kagō erōtēsō hymas (I also will ask you — the kai + egō is emphatic: I too, not just you, will ask) makes the exchange symmetrical and competitive. The counter-question is not evasion but a revelatory move: Jesus's question about John forces the authorities to reveal the epistemological framework that renders his authority question unanswerable.
4: To baptisma Iōannou ex ouranou ēn ē ex anthrōpōn — John's baptism — was it from heaven or from men? — the dilemma is perfectly constructed. Ex ouranou (from heaven) = divinely authorized (heaven is the Semitic circumlocution for God, cf. Luke 15:18); ex anthrōpōn (from human origin) = self-claimed without divine commission. John's baptism was universally acknowledged as morally powerful (Luke 3:10-14; Luke 7:29-30) and his credentials as a prophet were established. The question forces the Jerusalem establishment to take a position on divine authority — the same category in which Jesus operates.
5: hoi de synelogisanto pros heautous legontes hoti Ean eipōmen, Ex ouranou, erei, Dia ti ouk episteusate autō — they deliberated with one another saying: if we say, From heaven, he will say, Why did you not believe him? — synelogisanto (deliberated together — the compound prefix syn- indicates shared deliberation; logizomai = to calculate, reason; the council calculates its options). The first horn of the dilemma: affirming John's divine authorization immediately requires explaining why they did not respond to his baptism — a damning admission. Episteuein (to believe, trust, respond in faith) to John meant submitting to his call to repentance and his baptism.
6: ean de eipōmen, Ex anthrōpōn, ho laos hapas katalithasei hēmas — but if we say, From men, all the people will stone us — katalithasō (to stone, stone to death) — the crowd's conviction of John's prophetic status (Luke 3:15; Luke 7:29-30) made any denial of his divine authorization dangerous. pepeismenoi gar eisin Iōannēn prophētēn einai — for they are convinced that John is a prophet — pepeismenos (perfect passive participle: having been persuaded and remaining convinced) — the crowd's conviction about John is settled; it cannot be moved by the authorities' denial. The second horn is politically fatal.
7: kai apekrithēsan mē eidenai pothen — they answered that they did not know where it was from — the authorities' non-answer is an admission of epistemological paralysis or dishonest calculation. They do not say "we don't know" because they are genuinely uncertain; they refuse to answer to preserve their political position. The public non-answer in the temple courts, before "all the people" (Luke 20:9), is a public humiliation: the recognized religious authorities of Israel cannot or will not answer a question about John's authority.
8: kai ho Iēsous eipen autois, Oude egō legō hymin en poia exousia tauta poiō — and Jesus said to them: neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things — the response is symmetrical: they will not answer his question; he will not answer theirs. But the exchange has already answered the question: if they cannot evaluate John's authority (which Jesus implicitly identifies as the same category as his own), they lack the framework to evaluate Jesus's authority. The non-answer is the answer: Jesus's authority is from heaven, as John's was — the source they refused to acknowledge.
9: Ērxato de pros ton laon legein tēn parabolēn tautēn — he began to tell the people this parable — the audience shifts from the authorities (Luke 20:1-8) to the people (laos); the parable is told publicly, not privately. Anthrōpos ephyteusen ampelōna — a man planted a vineyard — the vineyard is Israel's classic OT symbol (Isaiah 5:1-7; Psalms 80:8-16; Jeremiah 2:21); the allusion is immediate for those familiar with the Isaianic "Song of the Vineyard." kai exedeto auton geōrgois — and rented it to farmers — geōrgoi (farmers, tenant-farmers) — the tenants who receive the vineyard on lease and owe the owner a share of the harvest; in the allegorical reading, the tenant-farmers are Israel's leaders responsible for the covenant community. kai apedēmēsen chronous hikanous — and went away for a long time.
10: kai en kairō apesteilen pros tous geōrgous doulon hina apo tou karpou tou ampelōnos dōsousin autō — at the proper time he sent a servant to the farmers so they would give him some of the fruit of the vineyard — en kairō (at the appointed time, the right time — harvest season) signals the legitimate expectation of return. The servant represents the prophetic emissaries sent to Israel throughout its history. hoi de geōrgoi exapesteilan auton deirantes kenon — but the farmers beat him and sent him away empty-handed — deirō (to beat, flay — the word for flogging; the same used in Luke 12:47-48 for punishment) and kenon (empty — without the fruit owed). The pattern of prophetic rejection: sent, beaten, dismissed.
11: kai prosetheto heteron pempsa doulon, hoi de kakeinon deirantes kai atimasantes exapesteilan kenon — and he proceeded to send another servant; them also beating and dishonoring him they sent away empty-handed — prosetheto ... pempsa (he added, he proceeded to send — the idiomatic Lukan construction for sequential action; cf. Luke 20:12). Atimasantes (dishonoring — treating with contempt, stripping of honor) adds a dimension beyond physical beating: the servant is publicly shamed, a serious violation in an honor-shame culture. The prophetic-rejection pattern continues: each emissary receives worse treatment.
12: kai prosetheto triton pempsa, hoi de kai touton traumatisantes exebalon — he proceeded to send a third, and they also wounding this one threw him out — traumatizō (to wound — from trauma, wound; a medical term Luke employs precisely) is a more severe injury than beating (deirō in Luke 20:10-11); escalating violence. Exebalon (threw him out, cast him outside) removes him from the vineyard itself — the escalation from dismissal to expulsion. The three-servant sequence recalls the three-year search for fruit (Luke 13:7) and generally represents the full prophetic history of Israel.
13: eipen de ho kyrios tou ampelōnos, Ti poiēsō — the lord of the vineyard said: what shall I do? — the owner's self-deliberation (an internal monologue, as in the Rich Fool Luke 12:17) reveals not weakness but the extraordinary patience of one who has extended every reasonable option. Pempsō ton huion mou ton agapēton — I will send my beloved son — ton huion ton agapēton (the beloved son) — the exact phrase from the baptism voice (Luke 3:22: su ei ho huios mou ho agapētos) and the Transfiguration (Luke 9:35: ho huios mou ho eklelegmenos). The allegorical identification is transparent: the owner's beloved son is the Son whom God sent. Isōs touton entrapēsontai — perhaps they will respect him (entrepō: to turn toward, show regard for, feel shame before).
14: idontes de auton hoi geōrgoi dielogizonto pros allēlous legontes, Houtos estin ho klēronomos — seeing him, the farmers deliberated with one another: this is the heir — dielogizonto (deliberated, reasoned among themselves — the sinister planning of those who recognize the heir and see an opportunity). Klēronomos (heir, inheritor — the one who will inherit the estate upon the owner's death) — the tenants' recognition of the heir is paradoxical: knowing who he is does not produce submission but determines the plan of murder. apokteinōmen auton, hina hēmōn genētai hē klēronomia — let us kill him so that the inheritance may become ours — the logic of seizure: remove the heir, become heirs yourselves. The greed of the tenants has become murderous.
15: kai ekbalontes auton exō tou ampelōnos apekteinan — throwing him out of the vineyard they killed him — the sequence is significant: ekbalontes exō tou ampelōnos (throwing him outside the vineyard) before killing recalls Hebrews 13:12 ("Jesus also suffered outside the gate to sanctify the people through his own blood") — crucified outside Jerusalem's walls. The allegory maps precisely: the son killed outside the vineyard = the Messiah crucified outside the city. ti oun poiēsei autois ho kyrios tou ampelōnos — what then will the lord of the vineyard do to them? — the question Jesus poses to the audience, inviting them to judge the tenants and thereby judge themselves.
16: eleusetai kai apolesei tous geōrgous toutous kai dōsei ton ampelōna allois — he will come and destroy those farmers and give the vineyard to others — the judgment on the tenants (the Jerusalem leadership) and the transfer of stewardship ("give to others") points to the post-resurrection mission: the vineyard's management passes from the temple establishment to the apostolic community. akousantes de eipan, Mē genoito — hearing this, they said: may it never be! — Mē genoito (may it not happen! — the strongest Greek negative wish, Paul's characteristic "by no means!" in Romans; LXX: mē genoito for "God forbid"). The audience recoils from the judgment they have just endorsed.
17: ho de emblepsas autois eipen, Ti oun estin to gegrammenon touto — looking at them he said: what then is this that is written? — emblepsas (looking intently, fixing his gaze on them) — the penetrating gaze that follows their protest. Lithon hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomomountes, houtos egenēthē eis kephalēn gōnias — The stone which the builders rejected, this became the cornerstone — Psalms 118:22 (LXX) — the rejection-of-the-builder's-stone is a wisdom paradox: the builders are experts in judging stone quality, yet the stone they discarded as unsuitable becomes the kephalē gōnias (head/top of the corner — either the cornerstone at the foundation or the capstone at the top of an arch; either way, the structurally critical stone). The Psalm citation was already used in Luke 20:38 (Psalms 118:26); Jesus uses Psalm 118 as a unity.
18: pas ho pesōn ep ekeinon ton lithon synthlastesetai — everyone falling on that stone will be shattered — synthlō (to shatter, crush to pieces) — the stone that is the cornerstone is also a stone of stumbling (cf. Isaiah 8:14; Romans 9:32-33; 1 Peter 2:8). eph hon d an pesē, likmēsei auton — and on whomever it falls, it will crush him to powder — likmaō (to winnow, scatter like chaff; then, to grind to powder) — the image moves from stumbling to being crushed by a falling stone; both describe the catastrophic consequences of rejecting the Messiah-cornerstone. Daniel 2:44 (the stone that grinds the statue to powder) is the background.
19: kai ezētēsan hoi grammateis kai hoi archiereis epibalein ep auton tas cheiras en autē tē hōra — the scribes and chief priests sought to lay hands on him in that very hour — the authorities recognize themselves as the tenants of the parable; the immediate consequence is intensified desire for arrest. En autē tē hōra (in that very hour) — the recognition of the allegory's target produces instant hostility. kai ephobēthēsan ton laon — and they feared the people — the same crowd-protection as Luke 19:48; the popular regard for Jesus prevents open action. egnōsan gar hoti pros autous tēn parabolēn tautēn eipen — for they knew that he spoke this parable against them.
20: kai paratērēsantes apesteilan enkathetus hypokrinoumenous heautous dikaiious einai — watching closely they sent spies pretending to be righteous — enkathetos (a spy, a plant in the audience — literally "one set in place secretly"; the only NT use of this word) underlines the deliberate deception. Hypokrinoumenous heautous dikaiious einai (pretending to be righteous — hypokrinō = to play a role, act; they perform righteousness while pursuing entrapment). hina epilabōntai autou logou, hōste paradounai auton tē archē kai tē exousia tou hēgemonos — in order to catch him in a word, so as to deliver him to the authority and power of the governor — hēgemon (governor — the Roman Prefect/Procurator; Pontius Pilate) — the goal is to get a statement that constitutes maiestas (Roman treason).
21: kai epērōtēsan auton legontes, Didaskale, oidamen hoti orthōs legeis kai didaskeis kai ou lambaneis prosōpon — they questioned him saying: Teacher, we know that you speak and teach correctly and do not accept personal appearances — ou lambaneis prosōpon (you do not take up a face = you do not show partiality, you do not play favorites; prosōpolēpsia is the NT term for favoritism) — the flattery establishes what they hope he will demonstrate: honest speech regardless of political consequences. all ep alētheias tēn hodon tou Theou didaskeis — but you teach the way of God according to truth — tēn hodon tou Theou (the way of God) is used later for the Christian movement itself (Acts 9:2; Luke 19:9,23; Luke 22:4; Luke 24:14,22).
22: Exestin hēmin Kaisari phoron dounai ē ou — is it lawful for us to pay taxes to Caesar or not? — phoros (tribute, direct tax — the provincial head-tax levied by Rome on subject peoples, distinct from the telos/toll which was indirect). The question is a political trap: answer "yes" and Jesus alienates the Jewish nationalist crowd who resented Roman tribute; answer "no" and he commits sedition against Rome. The Zealot movement had been founded on the principle that paying tribute to Caesar was treason against YHWH's sole kingship over Israel (Josephus, Ant. 18.23).
23: katanosas de autōn tēn panourgiav eipen pros autous — perceiving their craftiness he said to them — katanoeō (to perceive thoroughly, observe carefully — the same word for contemplating the ravens in 12:24 and the speck in Luke 6:41) applied to the detection of malicious intent. Panourgia (craftiness, unscrupulous cunning — from pan + ergon: doing everything/anything to achieve one's end; a morally negative term in biblical usage, cf. 1 Corinthians 3:19; 2 Corinthians 4:2) — Jesus names their scheme before addressing it.
24: Deixate moi dēnarion; tinos echei eikona kai epigraphēn — show me a denarius; whose image and inscription does it have? — dēnarion (a Roman silver coin worth a day's wage for a laborer; the standard wage in Matthew 20:2). The Tiberian denarius bore an obverse image (eikōn) of Tiberius Caesar with the inscription TI CAESAR DIVI AVG F AVGVSTVS (Tiberius Caesar, Son of the Divine Augustus) and a reverse image of the seated Pontifex Maximus. Eikōn kai epigraphē (image and inscription) — the coin carries both the ruler's portrait and his title. hoi de eipan, Kaisaros — they said: Caesar's.
25: ho de eipen pros autous, Toinyv apodote ta Kaisaros Kaisari kai ta tou Theou tō Theō — he said to them: then give back to Caesar what is Caesar's and to God what is God's — apodidōmi (to give back, return, pay what is owed — not merely "give" but "return what belongs to another") is the key verb: both Caesar's coin and God's image-bearers are to be returned to their rightful owners. The answer does not resolve the tension between political obligation and theological sovereignty but disarms the trap: using Caesar's coin implies operating within his economic system; operating within his system entails some obligation. The distinction between Caesar's sphere and God's sphere is left unstated — its content is the ongoing theological question.
26: kai ouk ischysan epilabesthai autou rēmatos enantov tou laou — they were unable to catch him in his word before the people — epilambanō (to catch, seize, lay hold of — here used for verbal trapping) — the trap failed. kai thaumasantes epi tē apokrisei autou esigesav — and marveling at his answer they fell silent — thaumazontes (marveling, astonished) indicates genuine surprise; the questioners had not anticipated the answer's elegance. Esigesan (they fell silent — aorist: a sudden complete silence) marks the end of this line of attack; the spies withdraw defeated.
27: Proselthontes de tines tōn Saddoukaiōn, hoi antilegontes anastasin mē einai — approaching, some of the Sadducees, who deny that there is a resurrection — Luke's parenthetical gloss on the Sadducees (antilegontes anastasin mē einai = saying in opposition that there is no resurrection) is a reader-orientation note for Gentile audiences unfamiliar with the Sadducean position. The Sadducees accepted only the written Torah (Pentateuch) as authoritative and rejected the Pharisaic oral tradition and doctrines of resurrection and angels (Acts 23:8). The resurrection debate places Jesus squarely in agreement with the Pharisees against the Sadducean aristocratic priesthood.
28: epērōtēsan auton legontes, Didaskale, Mōysēs egrapsen hēmin — questioned him saying: Teacher, Moses wrote for us — the opening invocation of Moses's written authority signals Sadducean method: scripture (specifically Torah) is the basis of the argument. ean tinos adelphos apothanē echōn gynaika kai houtos ateknos ē, hina labē ho adelphos autou tēn gynaika kai exanastēsē sperma tō adelphō autou — if a man's brother dies having a wife and he is childless, the brother should take the wife and raise up offspring for his brother — Deuteronomy 25:5-10 (levirate marriage, yibbum): the obligation of the surviving brother to marry the widow and produce an heir for the deceased brother's name.
29: hepta oun adelphoi ēsan — there were seven brothers — the number seven (hepta) is deliberately extreme: a maximally absurd scenario designed to expose the resurrection doctrine as logically incoherent within their reductio ad absurdum. The Sadducees construct a case that makes resurrection appear to violate Mosaic law (a woman cannot simultaneously be married to seven husbands). kai ho prōtos labōn gynaika apethanen ateknos — and the first married a woman and died childless — ateknos (childless, without offspring — from a- + teknon) activates the levirate obligation for the second brother.
30: kai ho deuteros — and the second — Luke's brief notation (vs. Matthew and Mark's slightly fuller version) of the second brother's marriage and death. The pattern is established by the first brother and repeated six times: marry, die childless, leaving the widow for the next. Luke's compressed account of brothers two through six (Luke 20:30-31) is characteristic of his editorial economy when the point is established; the reader understands the pattern without full repetition.
31: kai ho tritos elaben autēn, hōsautōs de kai hoi hepta ou katélipon tekna kai apethanon — the third took her, and likewise also all seven left no children and died — hōsautōs (in the same way, likewise) abbreviates the repetition; the pattern holds through all seven brothers. Ou katelipon tekna (left no children) — the levirate obligation was never fulfilled; no heir was produced. The scenario is designed to be maximally embarrassing for resurrection doctrine: if she is resurrected, she simultaneously belongs to seven husbands under Moses's law.
32: hysteron kai hē gynē apethanen — finally the woman also died — the widow's death (last in the sequence) completes the scenario. All eight principals (seven brothers + the widow) have died, creating the theoretical resurrection problem. The Sadducean question assumes: (a) resurrection involves resurrection of the same relational configuration as earthly life, and (b) therefore levirate marriage creates an insoluble legal problem in the resurrection. Both assumptions Jesus will correct.
33: hē gynē oun en tē anastasei, tinos autōn ginetai gynē — in the resurrection therefore, whose wife will she be? for the seven had her as wife — the question is the payoff of the elaborate scenario: tinos autōn ginetai gynē (of which of them does she become wife? — the present ginetai makes it an ongoing relational question, not merely a legal technicality). The scenario assumes that resurrection implies continuation of earthly legal statuses; the answer will challenge this assumption fundamentally.
34: kai eipen autois ho Iēsous, Hoi huioi tou aiōnos toutou gamousin kai gamiskontai — Jesus said to them: the sons of this age marry and are given in marriage — hoi huioi tou aiōnos toutou (the sons of this age — Lukan term for those whose life is defined by the present age, as opposed to the sons of the coming age in Luke 20:35). Gamousin kai gamiskontai (marry and are given in marriage — the active and passive of the wedding: men take wives; women are given by fathers) — marriage is the institutional structure of the present mortal age, necessary for biological continuity through death.
35: hoi de kataxiōthentes tou aiōnos ekeinou tychein kai tēs anastaseōs tēs ek nekrōn — but those who are counted worthy of that age and of the resurrection from the dead — kataxiōthentes (those counted worthy, deemed worthy — the divine passive indicating God's judgment of fitness for the age to come) — the resurrection life is not automatic or universal in Jesus's framing but a qualification (axios = worthy; the same root as in Matthew 10:37-38). oute gamousin oute gamizontai — neither marry nor are given in marriage — the resurrection life does not require marriage because its biological-continuity function (producing offspring to carry on after death) is rendered obsolete when death itself is abolished (Luke 20:36).
36: oude gar apothanein eti dynantai, isangeloi gar eisin kai huioi eisin Theou tēs anastaseōs huioi ontes — for they can no longer die; for they are like angels and are sons of God, being sons of the resurrection — isangeloi (like/equal to angels — a compound hapax in the NT; the only NT description of the resurrection state as angel-like) — not that humans become angels but that they share the angels' deathlessness. Apothanein eti dynantai (they can no longer die) — the abolition of death (1 Corinthians 15:26; Revelation 21:4) removes the need for marriage's biological function. Huioi Theou (sons of God) — in the resurrection, the divine-adoption that believers receive now (Romans 8:14-17; Galatians 4:6-7) reaches its fullness.
37: hoti de egeirontai hoi nekroi, kai Mōysēs emēnusen epi tēs batou, hōs legei Kyrion ton Theon Abraam kai Theon Isaak kai Theon Iakōb — that the dead are raised, even Moses showed in the passage about the burning bush, as he calls the Lord the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — Jesus uses Sadducean methodology against them: they accept only Moses; Moses himself teaches resurrection. The burning bush passage is Exodus 3:6. The argument is grammatical and theological: YHWH identifies himself in the present tense (egō eimi) as the God of three patriarchs who were already long dead by Moses's time. YHWH's self-identification with the living patriarchs implies they are alive to him.
38: theos de ouk estin nekrōn alla zōntōn, pantes gar autō zōsin — he is not the God of the dead but of the living, for all are alive to him — the theological principle derived from Exodus 3:6: YHWH's covenant relationship with the patriarchs is a present reality, not merely a historical record. Pantes gar autō zōsin (for all are alive to him) — the phrase may mean "all will be alive in his sight at the resurrection" (proleptic) or "all are already alive in his perspective" (eternal present); both support the resurrection. The argument is from the nature of God as covenant-keeper: a God who identifies himself by his relationship with people maintains that relationship, including through death.
39: apokrithentes de tines tōn grammateōn eipan, Didaskale, kalōs eipas — some of the scribes answered: Teacher, well said — the scribes' approval (kalōs eipas = you spoke well, beautifully) reflects the Pharisaic scribes' agreement with the resurrection teaching against the Sadducees. The cross-party dynamics of Jerusalem: the scribes (typically Pharisaic) enjoy Jesus's defeat of the Sadducees. The momentary alliance of scribal approval does not mean acceptance of Jesus as Messiah; it means the argument about resurrection was compelling and scored against a theological opponent.
40: ouketi gar etolmōn eperōtan auton ouden — for they no longer dared to ask him anything — etolmaō (to dare, have the courage — implying that questions now involved too much risk of public defeat; the authorities' line of interrogation has been exhausted). Three challenges have failed: the authority question (Luke 20:1-8), the tax question (Luke 20:20-26), and the resurrection question (Luke 20:27-38). Jesus has answered all three in ways that expose his questioners and silence further inquiry. The silence signals the shift of initiative: Jesus now asks the question (Luke 20:41).
41: Eipen de pros autous, Pōs legousin ton Christon einai Dauid huion — he said to them: how do they say the Messiah is David's son? — Jesus now takes the offensive with a Christological question about David's descendant. Pōs legousin (how do they say) — the impersonal "they" refers to scribal teaching; the expectation was that the Messiah would be a Davidic descendant (2 Samuel 7:12-16; Isaiah 11:1-10; Jeremiah 23:5-6; Ezekiel 37:24-25). This is not in dispute; what Jesus raises is whether "son of David" adequately describes the Messiah's nature and identity.
42: autos gar Dauid legei en biblō Psalmōn — for David himself says in the Book of Psalms — en biblō Psalmōn (in the Book of Psalms — Luke uses this fuller designation vs. Matthew/Mark's "David himself said in the Spirit"). Psalms 110:1 is the most frequently cited OT text in the NT (Matthew 22:44; Mark 12:36; Acts 2:34-35; Hebrews 1:13; Luke 10:13; 1 Peter 3:22; Revelation 3:21). Eipen Kyrios tō Kyriō mou, Kathou ek dexiōn mou — the Lord said to my Lord: sit at my right hand — Kyrios ... tō Kyriō mou — the LXX renders YHWH as Kyrios and Adonai also as Kyrios; David's "my Lord" (his Adonai) is thus addressed by YHWH himself, creating two divine referents.
43: heōs an thō tous echthrous sou hypopodion tōn podōn sou — until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet — Psalms 110:1b — the footstool image (hypopodion) is the posture of total subjection: conquered enemies placed under the feet of the victor (Joshua 10:24; Psalms 8:6). The waiting posture ("sit at my right hand until") implies an interim period between the enthronement and the completion of the subjection — precisely the period of the church's mission in Acts. The enemies' subjection is the Father's action, not the enthroned Son's; the Son waits and reigns while the Father acts.
44: Dauid oun auton Kyrion kalei, kai pōs autou huios estin — David therefore calls him Lord; how then is he his son? — the dilemma Jesus poses: if David calls the Messiah "my Lord" (Kyrios), the Messiah cannot be merely David's descendant in the ordinary sense (descendants were never "lord" over their ancestors in ancient Near Eastern culture; the social flow ran the other direction). The question does not deny Davidic descent (Luke's genealogy has just established it, Luke 3:31) but insists that Davidic sonship does not exhaust the Messiah's identity. Jesus does not answer the question; he leaves the Christological implication for the audience to resolve.
45: Akouontos de pantos tou laou eipen tois mathētais autou — while all the people were listening he said to his disciples — the public setting ("all the people listening") and the directed address ("to his disciples") creates a dual audience: the warning about the scribes is given to disciples but intended for the crowd to overhear. Pantos tou laou (all the people) is the full Jerusalem crowd that has been listening throughout the temple controversy.
46: Prosechete apo tōn grammateōn tōn thelontōn peripatein en stolais kai philountōn aspasmous en tais agorais kai prōtokathedrias en tais synagōgais kai prōtoklisias en tois deipnois — beware of the scribes who like to walk around in long robes and love greetings in the marketplaces and the first seats in the synagogues and the first places at banquets — three markers of honor-seeking: stolai (long, flowing robes — the distinctive dress of scribal scholars that signaled status; cf. Mark 12:38), aspasmoi en tais agorais (greetings/salutations in the public squares — public deferential address), prōtokathedriai (first/front seats of honor in synagogues) and prōtoklisiai (first reclining-places, the host's-right positions at banquets — the highest-honor dining positions).
47: hoi katesthiousin tas oikias tōn chērōn kai prophasei makra proseuchontai — who devour the houses of widows and for a pretext make long prayers — katesthiousin tas oikias tōn chērōn (devour the houses of widows) — the consumption of widows' estates while acting as religious advisors or executors of estates; a form of economic exploitation beneath a veneer of piety (cf. Isaiah 10:2; Ezekiel 22:25). Prophasis (pretext, cover, facade — prayer used as a reputation-building display rather than sincere address to God). houtoi lēpsontai perissoteron krima — these will receive greater judgment — perissoteron krima (more judgment, greater condemnation — the proportional accountability principle of Luke 12:47-48 applied to religious leaders who exploit their position). The scribal warning closes the Jerusalem controversy section.
